title: Explain Backpropagation Algorithm
description: Define backpropagation and explain how gradients flow backward through a neural network. Describe the chain rule application for computing partial derivatives with respect to each weight parameter.

title: Compare SGD vs Adam Optimizer
description: Compare Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) and Adam optimizers in terms of convergence speed, memory requirements, and typical use cases. When would you choose one over the other?

title: Explain Attention Mechanism
description: Explain the scaled dot-product attention mechanism used in Transformers. Describe what queries, keys, and values represent, how attention weights are computed via softmax, and why the scaling factor prevents gradient saturation in high-dimensional spaces.

title: Define CNN Architecture
description: Define the basic architecture of a Convolutional Neural Network. Explain convolutional layers, pooling, and how they extract spatial features from image data. Discuss stride, padding, and the role of fully connected layers in final classification.

title: Explain Regularization Techniques
description: Define L1 and L2 regularization. Explain how they prevent overfitting by penalizing large weights and what the geometric difference is between them. Discuss sparsity effects of L1 versus the weight decay behavior of L2.

title: Understanding Loss Functions
description: Explain three common loss functions: cross-entropy, mean squared error, and hinge loss. Describe when each is appropriate, what objective they optimize, and how the choice of loss function affects gradient magnitude and model convergence behavior.

title: RNN vs LSTM Comparison
description: Compare Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks. Explain the vanishing gradient problem and how LSTMs address it through gating mechanisms including forget, input, and output gates that control information flow.

title: Batch Normalization Overview
description: Explain batch normalization: what problem does it solve, how does it normalize inputs, and what are the benefits for training speed and stability? Discuss the role of learnable scale and shift parameters during inference.

title: What is Transfer Learning
description: Define transfer learning and explain why pre-training on large datasets then fine-tuning on task-specific data is effective. Provide one concrete example, such as using ImageNet features for medical image classification with limited labeled data.

title: Few-Shot Learning Basics
description: Explain few-shot learning: what is it, how does it differ from traditional supervised learning, and name two approaches (e.g., meta-learning, in-context learning). Discuss how support sets and query sets structure the learning problem.

title: Dropout Regularization Mechanism
description: Explain how dropout prevents overfitting during training. Describe the dropout probability parameter and how it affects network training vs inference. Discuss the relationship between dropout and ensemble learning, and typical dropout rates for different layer types.

title: Neural Architecture Search Intro
description: Briefly explain Neural Architecture Search (NAS): what problem does it solve, what are the main search strategies (e.g., reinforcement learning, evolutionary), and computational cost considerations.

title: Gradient Descent Variants
description: Compare Batch GD, Stochastic GD, and Mini-batch GD in terms of computational cost, noise level, and convergence properties. Discuss how mini-batch size affects gradient variance, memory usage, and the ability to escape local minima.

title: Activation Functions in Deep Learning
description: Name and explain four activation functions (ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh, SoftMax). Describe when each is used and advantages/disadvantages. Include the dying ReLU problem, saturation regions, and how Leaky ReLU and GELU address these limitations.

title: Overfitting and Underfitting
description: Define overfitting and underfitting. Explain the bias-variance tradeoff and strategies to diagnose and mitigate each. Discuss learning curves, regularization techniques, early stopping, and how model complexity interacts with dataset size to determine generalization.

title: Normalization Techniques Overview
description: Compare batch normalization, layer normalization, and instance normalization. For each technique, explain the axis of normalization, typical use cases (CNNs vs RNNs vs style transfer), and how the choice affects training stability and convergence speed. When would you choose one over the others?

title: Initialization Strategies
description: Explain why proper weight initialization matters in deep networks. Describe Xavier/Glorot and He initialization and why they prevent vanishing/exploding gradients. Discuss how each method accounts for layer width and activation function characteristics.

title: Classification vs Regression
description: Define classification and regression problems. Name one loss function and one metric appropriate for each. Explain how the output layer design differs between them, including softmax for classification versus linear activation for regression tasks.

title: Cross-Validation for Model Selection
description: Explain k-fold cross-validation: why it's better than a single train-test split for hyperparameter tuning and how it estimates generalization error. Discuss stratified k-fold for imbalanced datasets and typical choices for k in practice.

title: Embedding Layers in Neural Networks
description: Explain what embedding layers do, why they're useful for categorical data, and how they reduce dimensionality while capturing semantic relationships. Discuss how embeddings are learned during training and their use in word representations and recommendation systems.

title: Advanced Backpropagation Analysis
description: You have a 4-layer feedforward network with ReLU activations and squared error loss. The gradient for weight w2 in layer 2 is computed as ∂L/∂w2 = (∂L/∂y)(∂y/∂o3)(∂o3/∂o2)(∂o2/∂w2). Explain why certain intermediate terms might vanish and how residual connections or skip connections mitigate this.

title: Design Optimizer for Sparse Data
description: You need to optimize a model for a task with sparse feature vectors and infrequent updates to different parameters. Compare why AdaGrad or Proximal SGD might be better than vanilla Adam for this scenario. What does each do to handle sparsity?

title: Multi-Head Attention Deep Dive
description: A Transformer layer uses 8 attention heads. Explain why multiple heads help capture different relationships. Design an experiment to measure if all heads learn distinct patterns or if some are redundant.

title: Analyzing CNN Filter Learning
description: After training a CNN on 100,000 images, you notice that many filters in early layers look similar (e.g., edge detectors). Is this redundancy a problem? Explain why it happens and whether you should prune similar filters or use regularization to encourage diversity.

title: Loss Function Landscape Analysis
description: Compare cross-entropy loss vs focal loss for imbalanced classification. Given a dataset where one class is 95% of data, explain how focal loss reshapes the landscape to focus on hard examples. Derive or explain the mathematical difference.

title: Dropout Rate Optimization
description: You're training a model and observing underfitting with dropout=0.2 but overfitting with dropout=0.5. Explain what's happening and design a principled approach to select the optimal dropout rate given validation data.

title: Meta-Learning vs Transfer Learning
description: Compare meta-learning (learning to learn) and transfer learning. When would you use MAML (Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning) over fine-tuning a pre-trained model? Provide a concrete scenario illustrating each approach's strengths and limitations.

title: NAS Search Space Design
description: You're designing a search space for NAS to find optimal architectures for medical image segmentation. What architectural choices would you include (e.g., layers, widths, skip connections)? How would you constrain the search to keep computational cost reasonable?

title: Gradient Checkpointing Trade-offs
description: Gradient checkpointing trades memory for computation time by recomputing activations during backward pass. Analyze when this trade-off is worth it. How does it affect training speed for different batch sizes?

title: Mixed Precision Training
description: Explain mixed precision training: why use FP16 for activations but FP32 for weights? Describe loss scaling and why it's necessary. What are the speedups vs numerical stability risks?

title: Curriculum Learning Strategy
description: Design a curriculum learning strategy for training a vision model on images of varying complexity (simple geometric shapes → complex real-world scenes). Explain how to order examples and adapt difficulty progression.

title: Knowledge Distillation Pipeline
description: You have a large teacher model (1B params) and want to distill it to a small student (50M params) for deployment. Design the distillation process: what is the student trained to mimic (hard targets, soft targets, both)? How do you weight the two objectives?

title: Adversarial Training Analysis
description: Explain adversarial training for robustness: why it makes models more robust but sometimes hurts clean accuracy. Design an experiment to measure the clean vs robust accuracy frontier.

title: Explainability via Attention Visualization
description: Using a Transformer model, you want to explain which tokens the model attended to when making a prediction. Design a visualization and analysis strategy to interpret attention patterns. How do you distinguish genuine reasoning from noise?

title: Data Augmentation Strategy Design
description: For a medical imaging task (X-rays), design a comprehensive data augmentation pipeline. Which augmentations are safe (preserve diagnostic information) vs risky? How do you validate that augmentation doesn't introduce bias?

title: Hyperparameter Interaction Effects
description: Learning rate and batch size have complex interactions (you can often increase LR proportionally to batch size). Explain this interaction theoretically (noise scale, gradient magnitude) and design an experiment to find the optimal (batch_size, lr) pairs.

title: Continual Learning Without Catastrophic Forgetting
description: A model is trained on Task A, then fine-tuned on Task B. It forgets Task A performance (catastrophic forgetting). Name two approaches to prevent this (e.g., elastic weight consolidation, replay buffers) and compare their computational/memory costs.

title: Model Pruning vs Quantization
description: Compare weight pruning (removing small weights) and quantization (lower bit precision) for model compression. Which is better for inference latency vs accuracy? Can you combine them?

title: Ensemble Methods Beyond Voting
description: Simple ensemble voting often improves accuracy. Design a more sophisticated ensemble strategy: how would you weight models, combine predictions for regression, or use stacking to train a meta-model?

title: Class Imbalance Mitigation Comparative Analysis
description: A classification task has 1% positive examples. Compare oversampling, undersampling, weighted loss, threshold adjustment, and cost-sensitive learning. For each, explain the bias-variance implications and computational cost.

title: System Design: Image Classification Pipeline
description: Design a complete image classification system from scratch for a startup. Include: data preprocessing, model architecture choice, training strategy (single vs ensemble), deployment considerations (latency, memory), and monitoring for concept drift. Justify each design decision.

title: Scaling Laws and Compute Optimal Architecture Search
description: Recent work (Kaplan et al., Hoffmann et al.) shows that model size, training tokens, and compute budget follow power laws. Given a fixed compute budget C, how do you allocate between model parameters N and training tokens T? Analyze the Chinchilla scaling laws and design optimal model configurations.

title: Generalization Bounds and Empirical Risk Minimization
description: Derive or explain VC dimension, Rademacher complexity, or PAC learning bounds. Given a training set of size n, how do these inform what test error you can expect? How do regularization parameters (λ) relate to these generalization bounds?

title: Designing Federated Learning System
description: You're building a federated learning system where models train on edge devices without centralizing data. Design the communication protocol, aggregation strategy (FedAvg vs FedProx), and robustness against client dropout or adversarial clients.

title: Causal Inference in Deep Learning
description: Explain the difference between correlation and causation in ML. Design an experiment to distinguish correlation-based learning from causal learning using interventional data or causal graphs. How would you test if a model captures causal relationships?

title: Interpretable ML System Design
description: For a high-stakes decision system (e.g., loan approval), design a ML approach that is both accurate and interpretable. Compare rule-based systems, decision trees, linear models, and attention-based neural networks. How do you balance accuracy vs interpretability?

title: Benchmark Gaming and Robust Evaluation
description: Many models achieve high accuracy on standard benchmarks but fail in deployment. Analyze why (benchmark saturation, distribution shift, adversarial examples, spurious correlations). Design a robust evaluation protocol that better predicts real-world performance.

title: Multi-Task Learning Architecture Design
description: Design a multi-task learning system that learns 5 related tasks simultaneously. How do you handle conflicting gradients between tasks? Compare hard parameter sharing, soft parameter sharing, and task-specific adapters. When is MTL better than single-task?

title: Automating Hyperparameter Tuning
description: Compare random search, grid search, Bayesian optimization, and evolutionary algorithms for hyperparameter tuning. Design a meta-learning approach that predicts good hyperparameters for new datasets based on dataset characteristics.

title: Foundation Model Adaptation Framework
description: You have access to a large pre-trained foundation model. Design a framework for efficiently adapting it to 100 different downstream tasks with limited labeled data per task. Include parameter-efficient methods (LoRA, adapters), transfer learning strategies, and multi-task optimization.

title: Explain ReAct Framework
description: Describe the ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) framework. Explain how interleaving reasoning steps with actions helps agents solve complex tasks. Provide one example prompt that demonstrates the ReAct pattern.

title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting
description: Explain chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting and why it improves model performance on reasoning tasks. How does it differ from direct prompting? Give a concrete example of a problem where CoT helps.

title: Tool Use in Language Models
description: Define tool use for language models: what tools might an agent use, how does it decide when to call a tool, and how does it interpret results? Name three example tools.

title: AutoGPT Agent Components
description: Describe the key components of an AutoGPT-style agent: memory, planning, task decomposition, and execution. How does it iteratively refine tasks and handle failures? Discuss the role of self-reflection loops and external tool integration.

title: Planning Algorithms for Agents
description: Compare breadth-first search, depth-first search, and A* for agent planning. When would each be appropriate? How do you define heuristics for A*? Discuss computational complexity and how plan quality varies across these algorithms.

title: Memory Systems in Agents
description: Explain three types of memory for agents: episodic (what happened), semantic (facts and concepts), and procedural (skills). How should an agent use each type when solving problems?

title: Reflection and Self-Improvement
description: What is reflection in agents? How can an agent review its past actions, identify mistakes, and improve future behavior? Provide one concrete implementation approach, such as maintaining a reflection buffer that stores action-outcome pairs for periodic self-evaluation.

title: Function Calling and API Integration
description: Explain function calling: how an LLM decides what function to call, with what arguments, and how it handles the return value. Describe the JSON schema format commonly used.

title: Prompt Chaining Patterns
description: Define prompt chaining: sequencing multiple LLM calls where output from one becomes input to the next. Give two examples of chaining patterns and explain why it helps complex tasks.

title: Error Handling in Agents
description: Describe strategies for error handling in LLM agents: detecting errors, recovering from failures, retrying with modified prompts. What's a principled approach to error recovery? Include exponential backoff, fallback strategies, and graceful degradation patterns.

title: Agent Evaluation Metrics
description: What metrics would you use to evaluate an agent? Consider task success rate, step efficiency, cost, safety, and robustness. How would you measure each? Discuss the challenge of comparing agents across tasks of varying difficulty.

title: Tree-of-Thought vs Chain-of-Thought
description: Compare chain-of-thought (single path reasoning) with tree-of-thought (exploring multiple reasoning paths). When is branching exploration better? How do you manage the exponential cost? Discuss pruning heuristics and beam search as practical alternatives.

title: Grounding LLM Outputs
description: Explain why grounding (connecting LLM outputs to facts, tools, external knowledge) is important for agents. Name three grounding mechanisms and when each is useful. Discuss verification loops that check generated claims against retrieved evidence.

title: Agent State Management
description: How should an agent maintain state across multiple interactions? What should be stored (context, decisions, outcomes)? How do you balance completeness with context length limitations?

title: Uncertainty Quantification in Agents
description: How can an agent quantify uncertainty in its decisions? What mechanisms allow it to express confidence, request clarification, or defer to humans? Discuss calibration techniques, entropy-based thresholds, and when to escalate versus proceed autonomously.

title: Advanced ReAct Analysis
description: The basic ReAct loop is: Thought → Action → Observation → repeat. Analyze failure modes: when does an agent get stuck in loops? How would you detect and break infinite loops? Design a mechanism to force progression (e.g., limiting repeated thoughts).

title: Combining CoT with Tool Use
description: Design an agent that interleaves reasoning (CoT) with tool calls. When should the agent reason before calling a tool vs calling immediately? How do you prevent excessive reasoning or unnecessary tool calls?

title: Hierarchical Task Decomposition
description: An agent faces a complex goal: 'Write a research paper.' Design a hierarchical decomposition: top-level tasks, subtasks, and atomic actions. How does the agent decide which level to work at?

title: Memory Optimization for Long Tasks
description: An agent works on a task for 50 steps, accumulating 50k tokens of context. Context windows are limited to 8k tokens. Design a memory summarization strategy: what to keep verbatim vs summarize vs discard?

title: Reflection Mechanism Design
description: Design a reflection step for agents: how often should an agent reflect? What should it review (recent actions, success/failure patterns, goal progress)? How does reflection output modify future behavior?

title: Function Specification and Validation
description: An agent has access to 20 functions with complex signatures. Design a specification format (JSON schema, docstrings) that lets the agent understand what each does. How do you validate that the agent calls functions correctly?

title: Prompt Chaining with Branching
description: Design a system for chained prompts where some chains branch conditionally: 'If X, then do A-B-C, else do D-E.' How do you manage state across branches and merge results?

title: Robust Error Recovery Strategy
description: An agent encounters a parsing error from a tool (malformed JSON). Design a recovery strategy: retry with reformatted input, use a fallback tool, or ask for clarification. How do you prevent infinite error loops?

title: Multi-Agent Coordination Patterns
description: You want two agents to collaborate: Agent A focuses on research, Agent B on synthesis. Design a communication protocol: how do they share findings, resolve conflicts, and coordinate work?

title: Cost Control in Agentic Systems
description: An agentic system costs $1 per task if it works in 10 steps but $10 if it takes 100 steps. Design a cost-aware agent that trades accuracy for speed. How do you balance quality vs cost?

title: Safety and Alignment in Agents
description: Design safety guardrails for an agent system: preventing harmful actions, misuse of tools, and goal misalignment. What constraints, monitors, and overrides would you implement? Include permission hierarchies and human-in-the-loop approval gates.

title: Agent Behavior Logging and Debugging
description: An agent fails on 10% of tasks silently (produces wrong output without error). Design a logging and debugging system: what to log, how to identify failure modes, and how to trace the root cause?

title: Scaling Agents to Many Tasks
description: You need to deploy agents to handle 1,000 concurrent tasks. Design the infrastructure: task queue, agent pool management, result aggregation. How do you handle rate limiting and resource contention?

title: Dynamic Tool Selection
description: An agent has access to 30 specialized tools but using wrong tool wastes tokens. Design a mechanism for the agent to select appropriate tools: tool recommendation, cost-benefit analysis, or meta-reasoning about tool applicability?

title: Learning from Agent Failures
description: You have logs of 1,000 agent failures. Design a pipeline to: analyze failure patterns, extract insights, and update the agent system (prompts, tools, or parameters) to avoid similar failures.

title: Complex System Orchestration with Agents
description: Design an agentic system for a complex workflow: research legal precedents → analyze case details → generate legal brief → present to client with confidence scores. Include agent roles, communication, and quality gates.

title: Agent Behavior Prediction and Control
description: Given historical logs of agent behavior, design a method to predict what an agent will do next and intervene if needed. Use causal modeling or behavioral clustering to identify patterns.

title: Emergent Behaviors in Multi-Agent Systems
description: When you deploy multiple agents with simple individual rules, complex emergent behaviors arise (cooperation, specialization, conflict). Design experiments to measure and control emergence. How do you prevent unintended behaviors?

title: Agent Self-Improvement Without Retraining
description: Design an agent system that improves its own prompts, tools, or reasoning without retraining the underlying LLM. How do you ensure improvements generalize and don't overfit to specific tasks?

title: Adversarial Robustness of Agents
description: An attacker can manipulate tool outputs or provide adversarial prompts. Design defenses: input validation, anomaly detection, fallback mechanisms. How do you measure robustness? Include threat modeling and red-teaming evaluation protocols.

title: Agent Curriculum Learning
description: Instead of random task order, design a curriculum where agents solve easier tasks first, building skills for harder tasks. Define task difficulty, skill dependencies, and progression criteria.

title: Meta-Learning for Agent Prompting
description: Collect 100 tasks with their optimal prompts (found via grid search). Train a meta-learner to predict the best prompt for a new task based on task characteristics. How do you encode tasks and prompts for meta-learning?

title: Collaborative Agent Frameworks
description: Design a framework for agents to collaborate on a complex problem (e.g., scientific research): roles (observer, experimenter, analyst), communication protocols, conflict resolution, and consensus mechanisms.

title: Verifiable Reasoning in Agents
description: Design an agent system where all reasoning steps are verifiable by external validators. How do you generate proofs of reasoning? When is formal verification tractable vs intractable?

title: Adaptive Agent Architectures
description: Design an agent system that adapts its own architecture per task: selects planning algorithm, memory type, tool set, and reasoning depth based on task properties. Formalize task properties and architecture decisions.

title: Inverse Reinforcement Learning for Agent Goals
description: Given logs of successful agent trajectories, infer the underlying reward function the agent optimizes for. How do you handle ambiguity (multiple reward functions explain data)? Use maximum entropy IRL or Bayesian approaches.

title: Real-Time Agent Monitoring and Adaptation
description: Build a system that monitors agent health metrics (success rate, cost per task, error frequency) in real-time and adapts agent behavior (parameter tuning, prompt modification, tool pool changes) based on performance.

title: Agent Knowledge Graph Construction
description: Design a system where agents build and maintain a knowledge graph of entities, relationships, and facts encountered during task solving. How do you update the graph, resolve conflicts, and query it for reasoning?

title: Scalable Agent Execution Framework
description: You need to run 100,000 agent tasks simultaneously. Design a distributed execution framework: task distribution, agent process management, result aggregation, fault tolerance, and cost optimization.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Agent Improvement
description: Design a pipeline for iteratively improving agents with human feedback: collect trajectories, identify suboptimal decisions, gather human corrections, update agent policy. Balance human effort with improvement gains.

title: Agent Capability Benchmarking
description: You want to systematically measure what agents can and cannot do. Design a benchmark suite covering breadth (20 task types), depth (3 difficulty levels), and novel tasks. Define success criteria and measure sample efficiency.

title: Compositional Reasoning in Agents
description: Design an agent system that solves problems by composing learned skills from a skill library. How do you ensure skills compose correctly? Handle skill interactions and dependencies?

title: Counterfactual Reasoning for Agent Improvement
description: Given an agent trajectory, generate counterfactuals (what if the agent made decision X instead of Y). Use counterfactuals to measure decision impact and train agents on contrastive examples.

title: Privacy-Preserving Agent Collaboration
description: Multiple agents need to collaborate on tasks with sensitive data. Design privacy-preserving mechanisms: federated learning, differential privacy, secure multi-party computation. Trade-off privacy vs agent capability.

title: Agent Fairness and Bias Detection
description: Design a system to detect and mitigate bias in agentic decisions. What biases can agents develop (historical data, tool bias, prompt bias)? How do you audit fairness and ensure equitable outcomes across demographics?

title: RAG Pipeline Fundamentals
description: Explain Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG): how it retrieves relevant context before generation. Compare RAG to fine-tuning for knowledge injection. When is each better? Discuss how retrieval quality directly impacts generation accuracy and hallucination rates.

title: Dense vs Sparse Retrieval
description: Compare dense retrieval (embeddings + nearest neighbor) and sparse retrieval (BM25, keyword search). When is each better? Can they be combined? Discuss hybrid search approaches and how reciprocal rank fusion merges results from both methods.

title: Embedding Models for Retrieval
description: Explain what makes a good embedding model for retrieval. Describe MTEB benchmarks and metrics like nDCG and MRR. Name three popular embedding models. Discuss how training data diversity and contrastive learning objectives affect retrieval performance.

title: Query Expansion Techniques
description: Define query expansion: why it helps retrieval, and name three techniques (pseudo-relevance feedback, synonyms, semantic expansion). Give an example where expansion helps. Discuss how LLM-generated query reformulations can improve recall on ambiguous queries.

title: Vector Databases Basics
description: Explain vector databases (FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate). What indexing structures do they use (LSH, HNSW, IVF)? When would you use each? Discuss trade-offs between recall accuracy, query latency, and memory consumption for different index types.

title: Chunking Strategies for Documents
description: When indexing long documents for retrieval, chunking matters. Compare fixed-size chunking, sentence-level, and semantic chunking. What are the trade-offs? Discuss how chunk overlap, size tuning, and document structure awareness affect retrieval relevance.

title: Re-ranking and Cross-Encoders
description: Explain re-ranking: why retrieve many candidates (top-100) then re-rank to top-5? Describe cross-encoders vs bi-encoders. What's the computational trade-off? Discuss how cross-encoder re-ranking improves precision while maintaining acceptable latency for production systems.

title: Knowledge Graphs for Retrieval
description: How do knowledge graphs improve retrieval? Compare graph-based retrieval to embedding-based. When would you use each? Discuss entity linking, subgraph traversal, and how structured relationships complement unstructured text search for complex queries.

title: BM25 Algorithm Details
description: Explain BM25: the formula, parameters (k1, b), and why it works well for keyword matching. How does it compare to TF-IDF? Discuss document length normalization and how parameter tuning affects performance across different document collections.

title: Evaluation Metrics for Retrieval
description: Define nDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain), MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank), and MAP (Mean Average Precision). Why measure each? Which is best for different scenarios? Discuss how metric choice depends on whether users examine one result or many.

title: Hybrid Search Best Practices
description: Hybrid search combines multiple retrieval methods (dense + sparse, embedding + keyword). Compare RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion) vs learned combination weights. Discuss when fixed fusion rules outperform learned weights and how to tune the balance.

title: Cold Start Problem in Retrieval
description: When first deploying a retrieval system, there's minimal interaction data. How do you bootstrap: content-based features, transfer learning, or human judgments? Discuss synthetic query generation and using LLMs to create training pairs for initial retrieval models.

title: Personalization in Retrieval
description: How do you personalize retrieval systems? Consider user history, preferences, and context. Compare collaborative filtering, content-based, and hybrid approaches. Discuss privacy implications of user profiling and how to balance personalization with diversity.

title: Retrieval Feedback Loops
description: Explain how user clicks, dwell time, and explicit feedback train retrieval models. What's the difference between implicit and explicit feedback? How do you handle position bias?

title: Semantic Search Fundamentals
description: Explain semantic search: embedding both queries and documents, then finding nearest neighbors. Compare to keyword search. When is semantic better? Discuss failure modes where semantic search misses exact terms that keyword search would capture.

title: Advanced RAG Architecture
description: Design an advanced RAG system: multi-stage retrieval (retrieve docs → sentences → facts), adaptive retrieval (query-dependent top-k), and multi-hop reasoning where retrieved context triggers more retrievals.

title: Embedding Space Analysis
description: You notice that your embedding model clusters documents by date instead of topic. Analyze why (temporal bias in training data). Design experiments to measure and correct this.

title: Query Expansion Strategy Design
description: Design a comprehensive query expansion system: analyze query intent (yes/no question vs fact-seeking), use pseudo-relevance feedback to identify related terms, and weight original vs expanded terms.

title: Vector Database Index Optimization
description: You're indexing 100M documents. Compare HNSW, IVF, and SCANN in terms of memory, search speed, and accuracy. Design an indexing strategy for your constraints. Include quantization techniques like product quantization to reduce memory footprint.

title: Semantic Chunking Strategy
description: Design a semantic chunking algorithm: detect natural boundaries in text (paragraph breaks, topic shifts, section headers) and chunk accordingly. How do you handle cross-chunk dependencies?

title: Learned Ranking Pipeline
description: Design a learning-to-rank (LTR) system: feature engineering (query-doc relevance, retrieval scores, document quality), training data collection, and ranking model training. What model would you use?

title: Handling Retrieval Failures
description: Your retrieval system sometimes returns irrelevant context, degrading generation quality. Design detection mechanisms: relevance thresholding, consistency checking, or semantic similarity gating. Include confidence scores and fallback strategies when no relevant passage is found.

title: Knowledge Graph Embedding for Retrieval
description: You have a knowledge graph (entities, relationships). Design embeddings that capture both entity semantics and relationship structure. Use techniques like TransE or GCN. How do you query the graph?

title: Multimodal Retrieval System
description: Design a retrieval system for multimodal data (text, images, tables). Use aligned embeddings (CLIP-style) that project different modalities into a shared space. How do you handle modality-specific features?

title: Temporal Awareness in Retrieval
description: Retrieval systems should prefer recent documents but also value classics. Design a temporal weighting scheme: decay old documents but allow high-quality classics to rank highly.

title: Domain Adaptation for Embeddings
description: You're using a general-purpose embedding model (trained on web data) but need to retrieve scientific papers. Design a domain adaptation pipeline: fine-tune embeddings on in-domain data, evaluate on domain-specific benchmarks.

title: Retrieval at Scale with Constraints
description: Design retrieval for 1B documents with <100ms latency and <1GB memory per server. What indexing strategy, quantization, and distribution would you use? Discuss sharding approaches, approximate nearest neighbor trade-offs, and caching strategies for frequent queries.

title: Diversity in Retrieval Results
description: Simple retrieval often returns similar documents (all discussing the same aspect). Design diversity-aware retrieval: measure diversity, select diverse subsets (maximal marginal relevance, determinantal point processes).

title: Retrieval for Long-Form Generation
description: For generating long documents (research paper, novel), simple retrieval doesn't work — need multiple relevant passages from different sources. Design a multi-document retrieval system. Include iterative retrieval, query decomposition, and passage deduplication strategies.

title: Cross-Lingual Retrieval
description: Design a retrieval system that finds relevant documents across languages. Use multilingual embeddings (mE5, mBERT) or translation. Compare approaches. Discuss zero-shot cross-lingual transfer versus translate-then-retrieve and how language coverage affects quality.

title: End-to-End RAG System Optimization
description: You're building a RAG system for customer support: retrieve relevant docs → generate answer. Optimize the entire pipeline: retrieval speed, generation latency, accuracy. Measure end-to-end performance.

title: Adaptive Retrieval based on Query Complexity
description: Simple queries need simple retrieval, complex queries need multi-hop reasoning. Design a system that estimates query complexity and adapts retrieval accordingly (depth, number of hops, reranking rounds).

title: Retrieval with Explanations
description: Design a retrieval system that not only ranks documents but also explains why each is relevant. Generate explanations (query-doc similarity, keyword overlap, semantic connection). Validate with human judgment.

title: Handling Contradictory Retrieved Information
description: Retrieved documents sometimes contradict each other. Design a system to detect contradictions and synthesize conflicting information. When should the generator merge viewpoints vs ask for clarification?

title: Continual Learning for Retrieval
description: A retrieval system in production encounters new documents daily and user feedback on relevance judgments. Design a continual learning pipeline: update embeddings, rerank models, without full retraining.

title: Retrieval for Code Search
description: Design a retrieval system for code repositories: handle code-specific structure (functions, classes, patterns). Compare text retrieval, AST-based retrieval, and semantic code embeddings. Evaluate on code search benchmarks.

title: Privacy-Preserving Dense Retrieval
description: Design a dense retrieval system where queries and documents are encrypted (using secure multiparty computation or homomorphic encryption). Measure the privacy-utility tradeoff. Discuss latency overhead and how approximation techniques can make encrypted search practical.

title: Retrieval with Causal Reasoning
description: Some queries require causal reasoning (why did X happen), not just relevance matching. Design a causal retrieval system using causal graphs or counterfactual reasoning. Discuss how to identify causal passages versus merely correlated content.

title: Dynamic Weighting of Retrieval Dimensions
description: Retrieval systems typically use static weights (0.5*relevance + 0.3*freshness + 0.2*popularity). Design a system that learns query-specific weights using Thompson Sampling or reinforcement learning. Discuss exploration-exploitation trade-offs in online weight adaptation.

title: Evaluating Retrieval System Robustness
description: Design a robustness evaluation framework for retrieval systems: adversarial queries, distribution shift, adversarial documents. Measure performance degradation and design defenses. Include benchmark construction with systematically perturbed queries and poisoned document sets.

title: Meta-Learning for Retrieval Hyperparameters
description: Retrieval systems have many hyperparameters (weights, chunk size, rerank cutoff). Collect a dataset of (task_metadata, optimal_hyperparameters) and train a meta-learner to predict hyperparameters for new tasks.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Fine-Tuning
description: Instead of fine-tuning a model directly, use retrieval during fine-tuning to inject task-relevant context. Design a curriculum: early epochs use broad retrieval, later epochs use targeted retrieval.

title: Graph Neural Networks for Retrieval
description: Design a GNN-based retrieval system where documents are nodes, edges represent citations/references. Use GNN to propagate relevance signals through the graph. How does performance compare to non-graph retrieval?

title: Retrieval System Fairness and Bias
description: Analyze potential biases in retrieval systems: does the system retrieve documents about certain demographics less frequently? Design fairness metrics and mitigation strategies. Discuss re-ranking for demographic parity and auditing embedding spaces for representational bias.

title: Zero-Shot Retrieval Generalization
description: Train a retrieval system on one domain (news articles), then test on a new domain (scientific papers) without fine-tuning. Measure generalization. Design domain-agnostic retrieval architectures.

title: Instruction-Tuned Retrieval Models
description: Design a retrieval system where both queries and documents are conditioned on task instructions (e.g., 'Find documents relevant for summarization' vs 'Find documents relevant for question answering'). Train instruction-tuned embeddings.

title: Retrieval for Fact Verification
description: Design a retrieval system that finds evidence to support/refute claims. Compare retrieval for evidence vs retrieval for general relevance. How do you validate retrieved facts?

title: Efficient Dense Retrieval for Mobile
description: Design a dense retrieval system that runs on mobile devices (limited memory, CPU). Use quantization, distillation, and lightweight embeddings. Trade accuracy for efficiency. Discuss model pruning and how on-device caching can reduce repeated computation.

title: Retrieval Interaction Effects
description: Some queries have synergistic interactions (query A + query B together retrieve better results than separately). Design a system that detects and exploits query interactions.

title: Benchmark Development for Advanced Retrieval
description: Design a benchmark that goes beyond keyword matching: requires semantic understanding, multi-hop reasoning, temporal reasoning, and real-world difficulty. Collect or generate 500+ examples, collect human annotations, and establish baselines.

title: Agent Roles and Specialization
description: In a multi-agent system, agents can have different roles (researcher, synthesizer, critic). Explain how role specialization improves collective performance. Give one example. Discuss how dynamic role switching based on task demands can further boost team effectiveness.

title: Agent Communication Protocols
description: How do agents communicate in multi-agent systems? Compare message passing, publish-subscribe, and shared memory. When is each appropriate? Discuss bandwidth constraints, latency requirements, and how communication topology affects coordination efficiency.

title: Task Allocation in Multi-Agent Systems
description: How do agents decide who does what task? Compare centralized assignment, decentralized negotiation, and market-based allocation. What are trade-offs? Discuss how task dependencies and agent capabilities influence the optimal allocation strategy.

title: Coordination Mechanisms
description: Name three coordination mechanisms in multi-agent systems (blackboard architecture, contract net, auction). Explain when each is useful. Discuss how problem structure, agent count, and communication constraints determine which coordination mechanism performs best.

title: Consensus Algorithms
description: How do agents reach consensus on decisions? Compare majority voting, weighted voting, and Byzantine fault-tolerant algorithms. When is each used? Discuss convergence guarantees and how network partitions affect consensus in distributed agent systems.

title: Conflict Resolution in Teams
description: When agents disagree, how do they resolve conflicts? Compare voting, negotiation, hierarchical override. When would each be appropriate? Discuss how conflict resolution strategy affects agent autonomy, decision speed, and overall system fairness.

title: Agent Scalability
description: How does multi-agent performance scale with the number of agents? Compare O(1), O(log n), O(n), and O(n²) communication protocols. Discuss bottlenecks in scaling and how hierarchical communication structures reduce overhead while preserving coordination quality.

title: Emergent Behavior
description: What is emergent behavior in multi-agent systems? Give two examples where simple individual rules lead to complex collective behavior. Discuss how emergence relates to self-organization and why predicting emergent properties from individual agent rules remains challenging.

title: Agent Learning from Interactions
description: How can agents improve by learning from their interactions with other agents? Compare centralized learning, federated learning, and decentralized learning. Discuss how interaction data provides richer training signals than isolated experience and the role of shared reward functions.

title: Debate Framework for Multi-Agent Reasoning
description: In a debate framework, agents argue for/against propositions. Explain how debate can improve reasoning compared to single agents. Give a concrete example. Discuss how adversarial argumentation surfaces hidden assumptions and reduces confirmation bias.

title: Hierarchical vs Flat Organization
description: Compare hierarchical (manager → workers) vs flat (all equal) agent organizations. What are the trade-offs in communication, responsiveness, and robustness? Discuss how task complexity and urgency influence which organizational structure yields better outcomes.

title: Shared Knowledge Bases
description: Agents working together often maintain shared knowledge. Design a system for maintaining consistency when multiple agents update knowledge simultaneously. Discuss conflict resolution via version vectors, last-writer-wins, or merge functions for concurrent updates.

title: Agent Accountability
description: In multi-agent systems, how do you track which agent made which decision? Design an accountability mechanism that enables auditing and debugging. Include structured logging, provenance tracking, and causal attribution for tracing decision chains across agents.

title: Advanced Coordination Protocol Design
description: Design a coordination protocol for 10 agents solving a complex task: define message types, decision points, and error handling. How do you ensure no agent blocks progress?

title: Task Allocation Optimization
description: You have 5 agents with different skills and 20 tasks with different requirements. Formalize the allocation problem (knapsack-style) and design an algorithm to assign tasks optimally.

title: Heterogeneous Agent Teams
description: Design a team of heterogeneous agents (different capabilities, knowledge, speeds). How does diversity help or hurt? Design team composition for complex tasks. Discuss complementary skill coverage, communication overhead, and optimal diversity metrics.

title: Communication Bandwidth Constraints
description: Agents have limited bandwidth to communicate. Design efficient communication protocols: what information is critical, what can be approximated, and how do you handle information loss?

title: Handling Agent Failures
description: In a team of 5 agents, one becomes unavailable. Design mechanisms to detect failures, reassign tasks, and maintain progress without restarting. Include heartbeat monitoring, task checkpointing, and graceful degradation when specialized agents go offline.

title: Multi-Agent Planning Algorithms
description: Design a planning algorithm where agents collectively plan how to solve a complex problem. Compare centralized planning, decentralized planning, and iterative refinement. Discuss how plan conflicts are resolved and how partial plans are merged into coherent strategies.

title: Debate and Voting for Accuracy
description: You want to use agent debate to improve decision quality. Design the debate format: what information agents share, how many rounds, how voting works, and how to detect collusion.

title: Incentive Design for Cooperation
description: Design incentives (rewards, penalties) that encourage agents to cooperate rather than act selfishly. Analyze game-theoretic stability: will agents maintain cooperation? Discuss mechanism design principles and how repeated interactions foster trust-based collaboration.

title: Knowledge Synthesis from Multiple Agents
description: Multiple agents provide partial information (each observes different aspects). Design a synthesis mechanism to merge observations into a complete picture, handling contradictions. Discuss confidence-weighted aggregation and how to detect and resolve conflicting reports.

title: Multi-Agent Goal Alignment
description: Agents may have conflicting goals. Design a framework for aligning individual goals with team objectives. When should you compromise individual vs team goals? Discuss Pareto-optimal negotiation and how utility functions can formalize goal priority trade-offs.

title: Emergent Specialization
description: In a team without explicit role assignment, agents naturally specialize (some become researchers, others synthesizers). Design mechanisms to encourage beneficial specialization. Discuss how reward shaping and task feedback loops drive agents toward complementary roles over time.

title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
description: Design a multi-agent RL system where agents learn policies that coordinate without central authority. Handle non-stationarity (other agents' policies changing). Compare centralized vs decentralized training.

title: Distributed Data Processing with Agents
description: Design a system where agents process data chunks in parallel, then aggregate results. Handle skewed data distribution, straggler agents, and result merging. Discuss load balancing strategies and how speculative execution can mitigate straggler delays.

title: Trust and Reputation in Agent Teams
description: Design a trust system for agents: track reputation based on accuracy/reliability, use trust to weight agent contributions, and update trust over time. Discuss decay functions, cold-start trust initialization, and how to prevent gaming of reputation scores.

title: Optimization for Multi-Agent Systems
description: Design an optimization algorithm for multi-agent tasks: each agent's objective affects others. Compare federated optimization, gradient sharing, and consensus algorithms. Discuss convergence guarantees and how communication frequency trades off with solution quality.

title: Scalable Multi-Agent Coordination
description: Design coordination for 1,000 agents. Traditional protocols have O(n²) communication. Design scalable alternatives using hierarchies, gossip, or approximations. Discuss how gossip protocols achieve probabilistic convergence and how cluster-based hierarchies reduce message complexity.

title: Testing and Debugging Multi-Agent Systems
description: Multi-agent systems are hard to test (non-determinism, race conditions, emergent behaviors). Design a testing framework: reproducibility, scenario generation, and bug detection. Discuss deterministic replay, chaos testing, and formal property verification approaches.

title: Large-Scale Coordination Protocol Design
description: Design a coordination protocol for 100+ agents working on a complex, long-running task. Include deadlock detection, failure recovery, and progress guarantees. Validate with simulations. Discuss timeout mechanisms and how to ensure liveness under partial failures.

title: Emergent Strategy Formation
description: Agents learn strategies through interaction. Design mechanisms to measure and encourage emergence of sophisticated strategies (e.g., deception, alliance formation, niche specialization). Discuss how population diversity metrics and strategy taxonomies enable tracking strategic evolution.

title: Agent Heterogeneity and Diversity
description: Analyze how diverse agent teams (different architectures, knowledge, reasoning styles) outperform homogeneous teams. Design diversity metrics and selection algorithms. Discuss ensemble effects, how cognitive diversity reduces blind spots, and optimal team composition strategies.

title: Byzantine-Tolerant Multi-Agent Systems
description: Design a system where some agents are adversarial (Byzantine). Use Byzantine fault-tolerant consensus algorithms. How many adversarial agents can you tolerate? Discuss the classic f < n/3 bound and practical BFT protocols like PBFT for agent coordination.

title: Optimal Agent Debate Format
description: Design an optimal debate format for 3 agents: Who speaks when? How is information shared? How is consensus determined? Use game theory and information theory to justify design.

title: Multi-Agent System Dynamics Modeling
description: Model multi-agent system dynamics as differential equations (agent populations, knowledge diffusion, cooperation levels). Analyze stability, equilibria, and transient behavior. Discuss how mean-field approximations simplify analysis when agent counts are large.

title: Information Aggregation in Prediction Markets
description: Design a prediction market where agents trade contracts based on beliefs about outcomes. Analyze whether market prices aggregate information efficiently. Compare to voting and other aggregation methods.

title: Multi-Agent Inverse Reinforcement Learning
description: Given multi-agent trajectories, infer the reward functions and constraints each agent optimizes. Handle partial observability and agent interdependencies. Discuss maximum entropy inverse RL and how strategic interactions complicate reward inference compared to single-agent settings.

title: Learning Communication Protocols
description: Design a system where agents learn to communicate efficiently without predefined protocols. Use emergent communication or multi-agent reinforcement learning. Measure protocol interpretability. Discuss how environmental pressure shapes message semantics and compositional language emergence.

title: Adaptive Organization Structure
description: Design a system where agents dynamically reorganize (hierarchy ↔ flat ↔ clusters) based on task demands. When is reorganization beneficial? Measure reorganization cost vs benefit.

title: Fairness in Multi-Agent Outcomes
description: Design fairness mechanisms for multi-agent systems: ensure all agents benefit from cooperation, prevent exploitation, and handle distributional fairness. Formalize and measure fairness. Discuss envy-freeness, proportional share guarantees, and how to audit fairness over time.

title: Multi-Agent System Verification
description: Design formal verification techniques for multi-agent systems: safety properties (agents never reach bad states), liveness properties (agents make progress), and robustness. Discuss model checking complexity and how compositional verification scales to large agent populations.

title: Scalable Knowledge Sharing
description: Design a knowledge-sharing system for 1,000 agents: efficient representation, query mechanisms, consistency maintenance, and privacy preservation. Measure communication overhead. Discuss distributed hash tables, content-addressable storage, and selective sharing based on relevance.

title: Multi-Agent Planning with Uncertainty
description: Agents plan collaboratively but face uncertain outcomes (actions fail, observations are noisy). Design robust planning: contingency planning, reactive replanning, and uncertainty propagation. Discuss how decentralized POMDPs model multi-agent planning under uncertainty.

title: Agent Meta-Learning for Collaboration
description: Train agents to collaborate with new teammates they've never met. Use meta-learning to develop adaptable collaboration strategies. Test on novel team compositions. Discuss how few-shot adaptation and theory-of-mind modeling enable rapid coordination with unfamiliar partners.

title: Conflict-Free Multi-Agent Consensus
description: Design consensus algorithms that always converge, even with Byzantine agents and incomplete information. Analyze convergence guarantees and communication complexity. Discuss randomized protocols, the FLP impossibility result, and practical workarounds for asynchronous networks.

title: Measuring Collective Intelligence
description: Design metrics for collective intelligence: wisdom of crowds, diversity bonus, coordination efficiency. Compare agent teams to centralized systems and human teams. Discuss how synergy effects emerge when team performance exceeds the sum of individual contributions.

title: Incentive Compatibility in Multi-Agent Design
description: Design multi-agent systems that are incentive-compatible: agents honestly report information and follow protocol without manipulation. Use game theory, mechanism design. Discuss the revelation principle, Vickrey-Clarke-Groves mechanisms, and strategy-proof allocation rules.

title: Temporal Coordination in Multi-Agent Systems
description: Design temporal coordination when agents have different speeds, communication latencies, and timing constraints. Handle synchronization, deadline coordination, and time-sensitive decisions. Discuss logical clocks, barrier synchronization, and how asynchronous execution affects plan coherence.

title: Long-Horizon Multi-Agent Task Execution
description: Design a system where agents collaboratively solve a complex, long-duration task (days or weeks). Include progress tracking, milestone planning, role evolution, and recovery from disruptions.

title: LLM-as-Judge for Text Quality
description: Explain how to use an LLM as a judge for evaluating text quality. Describe the rubric, prompt design, and how to reduce judge bias. What are failure modes?

title: Standard Benchmarks Overview
description: Name five standard ML benchmarks: MMLU, HumanEval, SQuAD, ImageNet, and one other. Briefly describe what each measures. Discuss how benchmark saturation drives the creation of harder evaluation suites and the limitations of leaderboard-driven research.

title: Human Evaluation Protocols
description: Design a human evaluation protocol: sampling strategy, annotator instructions, quality control, and inter-annotator agreement measurement. Discuss calibration sessions, annotation guidelines design, and how to handle edge cases where annotators legitimately disagree.

title: Inter-Annotator Agreement
description: Define Cohen's Kappa, Fleiss' Kappa, and Krippendorff's Alpha. When is each appropriate? How do you interpret agreement levels? Discuss how prevalence and marginal distributions affect kappa scores and when to prefer one coefficient over another.

title: Automatic Metrics for NLP
description: Explain BLEU, ROUGE, and METEOR. What do they measure and why? What are their limitations? Discuss how n-gram overlap metrics fail to capture semantic equivalence and why learned metrics like BERTScore offer improved correlation with human judgments.

title: Bias Detection in ML Systems
description: Describe methods for detecting bias in ML systems: stratified analysis, demographic parity, equalized odds. When would you use each? Discuss how intersectional bias analysis reveals disparities hidden by single-attribute fairness checks and the role of causal reasoning.

title: Robustness Testing
description: Explain robustness testing: adversarial examples, distribution shift, out-of-distribution data. Design a robustness test for an image classifier. Include perturbation budgets, attack methods like FGSM and PGD, and how to measure certified robustness bounds.

title: Calibration and Uncertainty
description: Explain calibration: when a model says 90% confidence, it should be right ~90% of the time. How do you measure calibration? How do you improve it?

title: A/B Testing for ML Models
description: Design an A/B test to compare two model versions. How do you determine sample size, randomization, and statistical significance? Discuss power analysis, multiple comparison corrections, and how to handle network effects in online experimentation.

title: Fairness Metrics and Trade-offs
description: Define fairness metrics (demographic parity, equalized odds, predictive parity). Explain why different metrics can conflict. How do you choose? Discuss the impossibility theorem showing that certain fairness criteria cannot be simultaneously satisfied except in degenerate cases.

title: Interpretability and Explainability
description: Distinguish interpretability (is the model transparent) from explainability (can you explain decisions). Name three explainability techniques. Discuss SHAP, LIME, and attention visualization, including their assumptions and when each provides faithful explanations.

title: Reward Hacking Prevention
description: Explain reward hacking: when agents optimize the metric instead of the true objective. Give two examples. How do you prevent it? Discuss reward specification challenges, the Goodhart's Law problem, and how multi-dimensional evaluation reduces single-metric gaming.

title: Cross-Validation Strategies
description: Compare k-fold, stratified k-fold, and time-series cross-validation strategies. For each, explain the assumptions about data distribution, when the strategy is appropriate (e.g., class imbalance, temporal dependencies), and common pitfalls like data leakage in time-series splits. When is each the right choice?

title: Statistical Significance Testing
description: Explain p-values, confidence intervals, and power analysis. Design a significance test for comparing two models. Discuss paired bootstrap testing, effect size measures like Cohen's d, and why multiple hypothesis correction matters when comparing many models.

title: Advanced LLM Judging Design
description: Design a sophisticated LLM-as-judge system: multi-dimensional evaluation (fluency, relevance, factuality), chain-of-thought judging, confidence scores, and bias mitigation. Discuss positional bias, self-preference bias, and calibration techniques for reliable automated judgment.

title: Custom Benchmark Design
description: Design a custom benchmark for evaluating agents on reasoning tasks: define difficulty levels, include diverse task types (logic, math, language), collect gold-standard annotations. Discuss contamination prevention and how to ensure the benchmark remains valid over time.

title: Scale Human Evaluation
description: You need to annotate 10,000 examples but human annotation is expensive. Design a strategy: sampling, crowdsourcing, expert/non-expert splits, quality control thresholds. Discuss active learning to prioritize uncertain examples and how to estimate annotation budget requirements.

title: Measuring Hallucination in LLMs
description: Design metrics and methods to measure hallucination (when LLMs generate false information). Compare factuality checking, consistency scores, and semantic entailment. Discuss how retrieval-grounded verification and self-consistency sampling help detect hallucinated content.

title: Sensitivity Analysis for Metrics
description: A model's performance depends on evaluation metric choice (BLEU vs METEOR for translation). Design a sensitivity analysis: how much do rankings change between metrics? Discuss rank correlation coefficients and when metric disagreement signals evaluation validity concerns.

title: Temporal Drift Detection
description: A deployed model's performance degrades over time (data distribution shifts). Design a drift detection system: metrics, thresholds, and retraining triggers. Discuss population stability index, Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests, and how monitoring windows affect detection sensitivity.

title: Adversarial Robustness Benchmarking
description: Design a benchmark for adversarial robustness: adversarial examples (FGSM, PGD), backdoor attacks, and poisoning. Measure both robustness and clean accuracy. Discuss the accuracy-robustness trade-off and how certified defenses provide provable guarantees.

title: Fairness in Multi-Class Classification
description: You have 10 output classes and 5 demographic groups. Design fairness metrics and mitigation: which fairness constraint (group fairness, individual fairness, causal fairness)? Discuss per-class calibration, threshold adjustment, and how to handle intersectional subgroups.

title: Error Analysis Methodology
description: Design a systematic error analysis pipeline: categorize errors (false positives, false negatives), root cause analysis, and failure mode taxonomy. Discuss how confusion matrix decomposition and error clustering reveal actionable patterns for targeted model improvement.

title: Measuring Agent Efficiency
description: Design metrics for agent efficiency: tokens used, steps taken, wall-clock time, compute cost. How do you compare efficiency across tasks of varying difficulty? Discuss normalized efficiency scores and Pareto analysis of quality-cost trade-offs.

title: Confounding Variable Analysis
description: Model A outperforms Model B on your benchmark. Design an analysis to check if B's poor performance is due to the modeling approach or confounding factors (data quality, hyperparameter tuning).

title: Few-Shot Learning Evaluation
description: Design an evaluation protocol for few-shot learning: vary sample sizes, measure learning curves, compare zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot performance. Discuss episode sampling strategies and how to report confidence intervals across varying support set compositions.

title: Long-Form Generation Evaluation
description: Evaluating long documents (essays, reports) with short metrics (BLEU) fails. Design comprehensive evaluation: structure, coherence, factuality, relevance. Combine automatic and human judgment. Discuss rubric-based scoring and how hierarchical evaluation captures discourse-level quality.

title: Negative Results and Publication Bias
description: Analyze publication bias: positive results are published, negative results disappear. Design a process to scientifically document negative results and ensure fair statistical reporting. Discuss pre-registration, funnel plots, and how file-drawer effects distort the scientific record.

title: Multi-Objective Optimization Evaluation
description: You want to optimize multiple objectives (accuracy, fairness, latency). Design evaluation: Pareto frontier analysis, trade-off visualization, and multi-objective metrics. Discuss hypervolume indicators and how scalarization methods convert multi-objective problems into tractable single-objective ones.

title: Evaluation in Domain Shift
description: Model trained on ImageNet is deployed on medical images. Design evaluation for domain shift: in-distribution vs out-of-distribution performance, adaptation methods. Discuss domain adaptation techniques like fine-tuning, domain adversarial training, and calibration under distribution shift.

title: Comprehensive LLM Evaluation Framework
description: Design an end-to-end evaluation framework for LLMs: multiple tasks (reasoning, knowledge, language, code), diverse metrics (accuracy, efficiency, robustness), bias detection. Create taxonomy of capabilities. Discuss how composite scoring prevents single-metric optimization.

title: Designing Unbiased Evaluation Benchmarks
description: Design a benchmark that resists gaming and cheating: diverse examples, no data leakage, hard negatives, adversarial examples. Validate that solutions must understand the problem, not memorize.

title: Human-LLM Evaluation Agreement
description: Investigate disagreement between human and LLM judgments: statistical analysis, error patterns, sources of disagreement. When should you trust human vs LLM judgment? Discuss annotation difficulty, subjective tasks, and how to calibrate LLM judges against gold-standard human labels.

title: Scalable Fairness Auditing
description: Audit fairness of a recommender system with billions of users/items: sampling strategies, group representation, fairness metrics, and interventions. Measure fairness violations post-hoc. Discuss scalable auditing pipelines and how to surface systematic disparities in recommendation exposure.

title: Causal Evaluation of Model Changes
description: You changed a model and metrics improved. Was it because of your change or confounding factors? Design causal inference methods: randomized trials, difference-in-differences, propensity score matching.

title: Detecting Shortcuts and Spurious Correlations
description: A model achieves high accuracy but uses spurious correlations (e.g., background patterns instead of objects). Design detection methods: saliency maps, causal analysis, adversarial tests. Discuss counterfactual data augmentation and how stress-testing reveals shortcut dependencies.

title: Metric Aggregation and Ranking
description: You have 15 different evaluation metrics. Design a principled approach to aggregate them into a single ranking. Handle metric weights, normalize scales, and detect conflicts.

title: Generalization Assessment
description: Test if a model truly generalizes or just memorizes. Design evaluation: hold-out test sets, domain shift tests, adversarial examples, and extrapolation tasks beyond training distribution.

title: Cost-Benefit Analysis of Evaluation
description: Comprehensive evaluation is expensive (annotation, compute, time). Design a cost-benefit analysis: which evaluation methods are worth their cost? When can you use cheaper proxies? Discuss how pilot studies and correlation analysis identify which expensive evaluations can be safely replaced.

title: Competitive Benchmarking Analysis
description: You have leaderboard rankings from 50 models. Analyze: what makes top models succeed (architectural choices, training data, hyperparameters)? Design a meta-analysis to extract insights. Discuss ablation studies and how controlling for compute budget reveals true architectural advantages.

title: Interpretability-Performance Trade-off
description: More interpretable models (trees, linear) often have lower accuracy than black-box models (neural networks). Design evaluation to measure this trade-off. When is interpretability worth accuracy loss?

title: Stress Testing ML Systems
description: Design comprehensive stress tests for deployed ML systems: adversarial inputs, noise injection, resource constraints, latency variations, and cascading failures. Discuss how chaos engineering principles apply to ML infrastructure and how to define acceptable degradation thresholds.

title: Meta-Analysis of Evaluation Methods
description: Compare different evaluation methods (LLM-as-judge vs human annotation vs automatic metrics) on the same 1,000 examples. Measure agreement, cost, and correlation with real-world outcomes. Discuss how meta-evaluation reveals which method is most reliable for each task type.

title: Temporal Evaluation for Continual Learning
description: A model learns continuously over months, encountering new data and tasks. Design evaluation: learning curves, forgetting measurements, forward/backward transfer, task interference. Discuss how elastic weight consolidation and replay buffers affect continual learning evaluation.

title: Reproducibility and Replicability
description: Design a framework for reproducibility: code/data release, hyperparameter documentation, random seed control. Measure how many results replicate independently. Analyze failure modes. Discuss containerization, dependency pinning, and how hardware variability affects reproducibility.

title: Backdoor Detection and Evaluation
description: Design evaluation to detect backdoor attacks in models: trigger patterns, targeted misclassification, and verification. Create adversarial benchmarks for backdoor robustness. Discuss neural cleanse, activation clustering, and how fine-pruning can remove backdoor triggers.

title: Privacy Preservation in Evaluation
description: Evaluate a model without revealing sensitive data: differential privacy for evaluation, federated evaluation, and secure computation. Measure privacy-accuracy trade-off. Discuss noise calibration in differential privacy and how privacy budgets constrain the number of evaluation queries.

title: Sample Efficiency Evaluation
description: Design evaluation to measure sample efficiency: learning curves with varying data sizes, extrapolation to infinite data, comparison of data efficiency across models. Discuss power-law scaling fits and how sample efficiency metrics inform data collection budget decisions.

title: Axiom-Based Evaluation
description: Define axioms that models should satisfy (consistency, transitivity, monotonicity). Design tests to verify axioms. When do models violate axioms? Can you enforce them? Discuss axiomatic evaluation as a complement to benchmark scores and how constraint enforcement affects model flexibility.

title: Comprehensive Research System Evaluation
description: Design a complete evaluation framework for a complex research system: task decomposition, independent component testing, end-to-end integration testing, failure mode analysis, comparison with baselines, and statistical reporting with effect sizes and confidence intervals.

title: Diagnosing Training Instability in Deep Residual Networks
description: A 152-layer ResNet with batch normalization is experiencing loss spikes every ~500 iterations during training on ImageNet. The learning rate is 0.1 with cosine annealing, and gradient norms oscillate between 0.01 and 100. Diagnose the likely causes, explain how skip connections interact with batch norm statistics during these instabilities, and propose a fix that addresses the root cause without simply reducing the learning rate.

title: Debugging Mode Collapse in Conditional GANs
description: A conditional GAN trained on a multi-label image generation task produces high-quality images for 3 of 10 categories but generates near-identical outputs for the remaining 7. The discriminator loss is near zero while the generator loss plateaus. Analyze the interplay between the conditioning mechanism, discriminator overfitting, and label imbalance in the training set. Design a multi-pronged fix incorporating spectral normalization, label smoothing, and architectural changes to the conditioning pathway.

title: Optimal Tokenization Strategy for Multilingual Scientific Models
description: You are pre-training a transformer model on a corpus containing English scientific papers, Chinese patent filings, and mathematical notation with LaTeX. BPE tokenization fragments mathematical expressions and Chinese characters while over-allocating vocabulary to English. Design a tokenization strategy that balances vocabulary allocation across languages and modalities, handles out-of-vocabulary mathematical symbols gracefully, and minimizes fertility (tokens per word) disparity. Analyze how tokenization choices propagate to downstream task performance in cross-lingual retrieval and equation understanding.

title: Memory-Efficient Training Under Heterogeneous Hardware Constraints
description: You have a cluster with 4 A100 GPUs (80GB), 8 V100 GPUs (32GB), and 16 T4 GPUs (16GB) to train a 13B parameter model. Design a training strategy that uses all available hardware efficiently, combining pipeline parallelism, tensor parallelism, and data parallelism appropriately across the heterogeneous nodes. Address load balancing when different GPUs have different throughputs, gradient synchronization overhead, and how to handle the memory disparity for activation checkpointing decisions.

title: Catastrophic Interference in Multi-Domain Continual Pre-Training
description: A foundation model pre-trained on general web text is being continually pre-trained on three sequential domains: biomedical literature, legal documents, then financial reports. After each domain, performance on earlier domains degrades by 15-30%. Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) reduces forgetting but caps peak domain performance at 85% of single-domain fine-tuning. Design a training regime that combines replay buffers with domain-specific adapter modules and progressive knowledge distillation to achieve less than 5% forgetting while maintaining at least 95% peak performance. Justify your memory budget allocation across replay, adapters, and base model parameters.

title: Theoretical Analysis of LoRA Rank Selection and Loss Landscape Geometry
description: When fine-tuning a 7B parameter model using LoRA, practitioners often choose rank r=8 or r=16 by convention. Analyze theoretically how LoRA rank interacts with the intrinsic dimensionality of the downstream task, the pre-trained weight matrix spectrum, and the loss landscape curvature. For a task where the optimal weight update has effective rank 12 but the pre-trained weight matrix has a sharp spectral decay after the top 50 singular values, determine whether r=8 or r=16 is better and why. Propose a method to adaptively select rank per layer based on gradient information during early training.

title: Diagnosing and Fixing Numerical Instability in Mixture-of-Experts Models
description: A Mixture-of-Experts transformer with 64 experts and top-2 routing experiences training collapse after 10K steps: the router converges to sending 90% of tokens to 2 experts while 50 experts receive near-zero traffic. Loss suddenly spikes when auxiliary load-balancing loss weight is increased. Analyze the interaction between router softmax temperature, load-balancing loss gradients, expert capacity factors, and FP16 precision. Design a fix that uses expert-choice routing, progressive capacity adjustment, and router z-loss to stabilize training without expert collapse.

title: Designing a Self-Supervised Pretext Task for Tabular Data
description: Unlike images (masked patches) or text (masked tokens), tabular data lacks natural spatial or sequential structure for self-supervised learning. Design a pretext task for a dataset with 200 features of mixed types: 80 continuous, 50 categorical, 40 binary, and 30 ordinal. The pretext task must capture inter-feature dependencies, handle missing values natively, respect feature type semantics (e.g., ordinal ordering), and produce representations useful for both classification and regression downstream tasks. Compare your approach to existing methods like VIME, SubTab, and SCARF, analyzing what structural assumptions each makes and where they fail.

title: Optimizing Inference Throughput Under Strict Latency and Accuracy SLAs
description: A production model serving system must handle 10,000 requests/second with p99 latency under 50ms and accuracy within 1% of the full FP32 model. The base model is a 3B parameter transformer. Design an optimization pipeline combining INT8 quantization, speculative decoding, KV-cache optimization, continuous batching, and model distillation. Analyze the accuracy-latency Pareto frontier for each technique, identify which combinations are synergistic vs redundant, and provide a deployment architecture that meets all three SLAs simultaneously. Address how to monitor for accuracy degradation in production.

title: Failure Analysis of Contrastive Learning with Distribution Shift
description: A contrastive learning model (SimCLR-style) pre-trained on ImageNet achieves 92% linear probe accuracy on ImageNet but only 61% on a satellite imagery dataset despite similar visual complexity. Analyze why the learned representations fail to transfer: examine how data augmentation choices (random crop, color jitter, Gaussian blur) create implicit inductive biases tied to natural images, how the contrastive loss function shapes the embedding geometry in ways that conflict with satellite image semantics, and how batch composition affects hard negative mining. Design a domain-adaptive contrastive pre-training strategy that bridges this gap without access to labeled satellite data.

title: Designing a Self-Healing Agent with Formal Verification Constraints
