description: An autonomous coding agent must modify a production codebase while guaranteeing that existing tests pass, type constraints hold, and no new security vulnerabilities are introduced. Design an agent architecture that combines symbolic verification (type checkers, static analyzers, SMT solvers) with LLM-based code generation, uses the verification feedback to guide iterative refinement, and maintains a causal model of past failures to avoid repeating them. Address the challenge of verification tool latency (30-60 seconds per check) and how the agent should parallelize exploration vs verification.

title: Context Window Management Under Adversarial Information Density
description: An agent processing legal contracts encounters documents where critical clauses are deliberately buried among boilerplate text, with key terms defined hundreds of pages before their use. The agent has a 32K token context window but documents average 200K tokens. Design a context management architecture that combines hierarchical summarization, attention-guided extraction, and a structured scratchpad to maintain semantic coherence across the full document. Address how the agent detects that important information was lost during compression, how it decides when to re-read sections, and how it handles cross-references between distant document sections.

title: Multi-Model Agent Routing with Capability-Aware Orchestration
description: Design an agent orchestrator that routes subtasks to different LLMs based on capability profiles: a large model for complex reasoning, a fast model for simple lookups, a code-specialized model for programming tasks, and a vision model for image analysis. The orchestrator must learn routing policies from task outcomes, handle model API failures with graceful degradation, manage a shared context across heterogeneous models with different tokenizers and context windows, and optimize for a combined objective of quality, latency, and cost. Analyze how routing errors compound in multi-step tasks and design a confidence-based fallback mechanism.

title: Designing Agents with Persistent World Models
description: An agent operating in a complex software environment (managing Kubernetes clusters) must maintain an accurate mental model of the system state that persists across sessions. Design an architecture combining a structured state representation (graph database of resources, relationships, and configurations), a learned transition model that predicts state changes from actions, and an anomaly detector that flags when observed state diverges from predicted state. Address how the world model handles partial observability, concurrent external modifications by other operators, and graceful degradation when the model becomes stale.

title: Bounded Rationality Agent Design Under Compute Budgets
description: Design an agent that operates under strict compute budgets (maximum 500K tokens per task, maximum 30 API calls) for complex research tasks that ideally require unlimited exploration. The agent must implement anytime algorithms that produce progressively better results, meta-reason about which subtasks deserve more compute investment, and dynamically reallocate remaining budget based on intermediate results. Compare strategies: fixed allocation across phases, UCB-style exploration-exploitation over subtasks, and learned budget allocation from historical task trajectories. Analyze how performance degrades as budgets tighten and identify the knee point.

title: Agent Memory Consolidation Inspired by Cognitive Architecture
description: Design a memory system for a long-running agent (operating over weeks) inspired by human memory consolidation: a working memory buffer (recent context), episodic memory (specific interaction records), semantic memory (extracted facts and patterns), and procedural memory (learned action sequences). Define the consolidation process that moves information between stores: what triggers consolidation, how episodes are generalized into semantic knowledge, how conflicting memories are resolved, and how procedural skills are extracted from repeated successful action patterns. Address memory capacity management and retrieval efficiency as the system scales to millions of stored items.

title: Designing Interpretable Agent Decision Traces for Regulatory Compliance
description: A financial services agent makes investment recommendations that must comply with regulatory requirements for explainability and auditability. Design an agent architecture that generates machine-readable decision traces linking each recommendation to specific data sources, reasoning steps, and regulatory rules consulted. The traces must be verifiable by automated compliance systems, support counterfactual queries (what would change if input X were different), and maintain audit trails across multi-step reasoning chains. Address the tension between LLM reasoning opacity and regulatory transparency requirements, proposing a hybrid architecture with constrained reasoning pathways.

title: Hierarchical Planning with Temporal Abstraction for Long-Horizon Tasks
description: An agent must plan and execute a 200-step software migration project (migrating a monolith to microservices) where actions have dependencies, some are reversible and others are not, and the environment changes between planning and execution. Design a hierarchical planner with three levels: strategic (milestone planning), tactical (task sequencing within milestones), and operational (individual action execution). Address how the planner handles plan invalidation when assumptions change mid-execution, how it decides when to replan vs adapt locally, and how temporal abstractions (grouping fine-grained actions into reusable macro-actions) are learned from experience.

title: Designing Agents Robust to Prompt Injection and Indirect Manipulation
description: An agent processes user-provided documents that may contain adversarial instructions embedded in seemingly innocuous text (indirect prompt injection). Design a defense architecture combining input sanitization, instruction hierarchy enforcement (system prompt > user instructions > document content), behavioral anomaly detection that flags when agent actions deviate from expected patterns, and sandboxed execution for potentially dangerous operations. Analyze attack vectors including multi-turn manipulation where each message is individually benign but collectively steer the agent, and tool-output injection where compromised APIs return adversarial content. Evaluate the defense-usability tradeoff.

title: Co-Evolutionary Agent and Environment Design
description: Design a system where an agent and its training environment co-evolve: the environment generates progressively harder challenges based on the agent's current capabilities, while the agent develops new skills in response. Formalize this as a two-player game between a task-generator and a task-solver, using ideas from adversarial training, curriculum learning, and population-based training. Address the stability challenge (preventing the generator from producing impossible tasks or the solver from gaming the generator), how to measure meaningful capability improvement vs superficial adaptation, and how to ensure the learned skills transfer to real-world tasks not seen during co-evolution.

title: Debugging Embedding Drift in Production RAG Systems
description: A production RAG system's answer quality has degraded by 20% over 6 months despite no code changes. Investigation reveals the embedding model was updated by the provider (version 2 to version 3), creating a mismatch between the query embeddings (v3) and the stored document embeddings (v2). Simply re-embedding all 50M documents is prohibitively expensive. Design a solution that combines embedding space alignment (learning a projection from v3 to v2 space), incremental re-embedding prioritized by document access frequency, and a hybrid retrieval fallback that uses BM25 when embedding similarity confidence is low. Analyze how embedding drift affects different query types differently.

title: Multi-Hop Retrieval with Evidence Chain Verification
description: A question-answering system must answer complex questions requiring information from 3-5 documents where no single document contains the full answer. Design a multi-hop retrieval system that iteratively retrieves documents, extracts intermediate answers, formulates follow-up queries, and constructs an evidence chain. Address the compounding error problem (each retrieval hop has 85% precision, so 4 hops yield only 52% end-to-end precision), how to detect when the evidence chain contains contradictions or circular reasoning, and how to verify the final answer is logically entailed by the evidence chain rather than hallucinated from plausible-sounding but incomplete evidence.

title: Retrieval System Design for Heterogeneous Enterprise Knowledge
description: Design a retrieval system for an enterprise with knowledge spread across Confluence wikis, Slack messages, Jira tickets, code repositories, PDF design documents, recorded meeting transcripts, and structured databases. Each source has different update frequency, access permissions, content structure, and reliability. Design unified indexing that preserves source-specific metadata, a query understanding layer that routes queries to appropriate sources, a fusion strategy that combines results across sources with source-reliability weighting, and an access control system that filters results based on the querying user's permissions without leaking information about restricted documents' existence.

title: Adversarial Document Injection Defense for RAG Systems
description: An attacker can inject documents into a RAG system's corpus that are designed to be retrieved for specific queries and contain subtly manipulated information. Design a defense system that detects adversarial documents using provenance tracking, statistical anomaly detection on embedding distributions, cross-reference verification against trusted sources, and temporal consistency checking. Analyze attack vectors including SEO-style keyword stuffing that games embedding similarity, Trojan documents that appear legitimate but contain targeted misinformation, and coordinated injection of multiple mutually-reinforcing false documents. Evaluate the false positive rate of your defenses on legitimate new documents.

title: Designing Retrieval for Temporal Reasoning Over Evolving Knowledge
description: A retrieval system must answer time-sensitive queries about evolving topics (e.g., 'What is the current CEO of Company X?' or 'What was the policy on remote work in 2022 vs 2024?'). Documents in the corpus span 10 years and may contain outdated information, superseded policies, or conflicting statements from different time periods. Design a temporal-aware retrieval system that indexes document timestamps and validity periods, understands temporal query intent (current state vs historical comparison vs trend analysis), resolves conflicts between documents from different time periods, and handles the challenge that the most embedding-similar document may be outdated while the correct current answer is in a less similar recent document.

title: Optimizing Retrieval Latency for Real-Time Conversational AI
description: A conversational AI system must retrieve relevant context within 20ms to maintain natural conversation flow, searching across a 500M document corpus. The system must handle multi-turn context (previous conversation turns influence retrieval), support real-time corpus updates (new documents added every second), and maintain retrieval quality comparable to a 200ms system. Design an architecture combining approximate nearest neighbor search with pre-computed conversation-aware query caches, speculative pre-fetching based on predicted conversation direction, tiered indexing (hot/warm/cold), and result pre-computation during user typing. Analyze where quality degrades and design a graceful degradation strategy.

title: Cross-Modal Retrieval with Alignment Uncertainty
description: Design a retrieval system where queries can be in any modality (text, image, audio, video) and documents can be in any modality, requiring cross-modal matching. CLIP-style aligned embeddings work well for text-image pairs but degrade for text-audio and image-video pairs where training data is scarce. Design a multi-stage alignment strategy that uses chain-of-modality bridging (text-image alignment + image-video alignment to get text-video alignment), uncertainty-aware similarity scoring that reflects alignment confidence per modality pair, and a fallback mechanism that transcribes or captionizes non-text modalities when cross-modal embedding confidence is low. Analyze error propagation through alignment chains.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Reasoning with Structured and Unstructured Knowledge
description: A medical diagnosis support system must retrieve from both structured knowledge (drug interaction databases, clinical trial results in tabular form, diagnostic decision trees) and unstructured knowledge (clinical case reports, research papers, physician notes). Design a hybrid retrieval system that translates natural language queries into both embedding-based searches over unstructured text and structured queries (SQL/SPARQL) over knowledge bases, fuses results across modalities with confidence weighting, and handles the challenge that structured data provides precise but narrow answers while unstructured data provides broad but noisy context. Address how to present the provenance of each piece of evidence to the clinician.

title: Self-Improving Retrieval Through Implicit Feedback Mining
description: A deployed RAG system generates answers that users sometimes accept, sometimes regenerate, and sometimes abandon entirely. Design a self-improving retrieval pipeline that infers relevance labels from these implicit signals (accounting for position bias, presentation bias, and the confound that users may accept wrong answers), trains a reward model for retrieval quality, uses the reward model to fine-tune the retrieval components (query encoder, re-ranker), and maintains an exploration mechanism to avoid convergence on local optima. Address the feedback loop risk where the system over-optimizes for user satisfaction metrics while actual answer accuracy degrades.

title: Retrieval System Architecture for Regulatory Compliance Document Analysis
description: Design a retrieval system for a compliance team that must cross-reference regulations across multiple jurisdictions (EU GDPR, US state privacy laws, industry-specific rules), track regulatory changes over time, identify conflicts between overlapping regulations, and retrieve the most specific applicable rule for a given business scenario. The system must handle hierarchical document structure (articles > sections > paragraphs > definitions with cross-references), maintain a regulatory dependency graph, support hypothetical queries ('If we expand to Germany, what additional requirements apply?'), and provide audit trails showing which regulations were considered. Address chunk boundary challenges when regulations reference definitions defined in other sections.

title: Designing Fault-Tolerant Consensus Under Partial Network Partitions
description: A multi-agent system with 12 agents experiences intermittent network partitions where agents can communicate with some but not all peers simultaneously, creating overlapping communication subgroups that change over time. Design a consensus protocol that maintains progress despite these partial partitions, detects when a partition has occurred (vs slow agents), handles the split-brain problem where two subgroups reach conflicting decisions, and efficiently reconciles state when partitions heal. Analyze the CAP theorem implications for your design and formalize the consistency guarantees under different partition scenarios. Compare your approach to Raft and PBFT adapted for this setting.

title: Multi-Agent Negotiation for Resource Allocation Under Incomplete Information
description: Five agents must allocate a shared compute budget across 20 tasks, but each agent has private information about task priorities that it may strategically misrepresent to gain more resources. Design a mechanism that incentivizes truthful reporting (strategy-proofness), achieves near-optimal allocation despite incomplete information, handles coalition formation where agents collude, and converges within a bounded number of communication rounds. Compare Vickrey-Clarke-Groves (VCG) mechanisms, proportional fairness, and Nash bargaining solutions. Analyze what happens when agents have different discount rates for future vs immediate resource access.

title: Emergent Division of Labor in Self-Organizing Agent Swarms
description: Design a system where 50 identical agents (same model, same tools, same initial prompt) must self-organize into specialized roles to handle a complex workflow (content creation pipeline: research, writing, editing, fact-checking, formatting). Without explicit role assignment, agents must discover and adopt roles based on task demands, develop expertise through experience, and dynamically rebalance when workload shifts. Formalize the emergence conditions using information-theoretic measures (mutual information between agent identity and role behavior), design mechanisms to prevent role starvation (no agent takes an unpopular but necessary role), and analyze stability of the emergent organization under perturbation.

title: Designing Trust Propagation in Hierarchical Multi-Agent Systems
description: A hierarchical multi-agent system has 3 layers: 1 coordinator, 5 team leads, and 25 workers. Trust must propagate bidirectionally: upward (workers report results whose accuracy affects team lead trust scores) and downward (coordinator delegates based on team lead reliability). Design a trust model that handles transitive trust (coordinator trusts worker through team lead), detects trust manipulation (a team lead inflating their workers' scores), accounts for task-dependent competence (an agent is reliable for math but not coding), and adapts trust scores using Bayesian updating with forgetting factors. Analyze cascading trust failures where one compromised team lead corrupts trust assessments for their entire subtree.

title: Multi-Agent Debate with Structured Argumentation and Epistemic Logic
description: Design a formal debate system where 4 agents argue about complex technical decisions using structured argumentation frameworks (Dung's abstract argumentation). Each agent constructs argument graphs with support and attack relations, and the system resolves conflicts using grounded, preferred, or stable semantics. Integrate epistemic logic so agents can reason about what other agents know and believe, detect circular arguments and fallacies automatically, handle the problem of LLMs generating plausible-sounding but logically invalid arguments, and determine when debate has converged vs when agents are simply restating positions. Compare debate quality to single-agent chain-of-thought on complex reasoning benchmarks.

title: Scalable Multi-Agent Simulation for Policy Testing
description: Design a simulation framework to test multi-agent policies before deployment at scale. The framework must simulate 10,000 agents with realistic communication latencies, model heterogeneous agent capabilities and failure modes, support accelerated time (running days of simulated interaction in hours), and generate adversarial scenarios that stress-test edge cases. Address the sim-to-real gap: how do you validate that simulation results predict real-world behavior? Design calibration experiments using small-scale real deployments. Include mechanisms for automated scenario generation using coverage-guided fuzzing of the agent interaction space.

title: Cross-Organizational Multi-Agent Collaboration with Competing Interests
description: Three organizations each deploy their own agent systems that must collaborate on a joint project (e.g., supply chain optimization) while protecting proprietary information. Design a collaboration protocol where agents share sufficient information for effective coordination but prevent leakage of trade secrets, competitive strategies, or internal capabilities. Use secure multi-party computation for joint optimization, design information-theoretic bounds on what can be inferred from shared messages, handle the incentive problem where organizations benefit from free-riding on others' efforts, and address liability when a jointly-made decision causes losses. Compare federated optimization, encrypted message passing, and zero-knowledge proof approaches.

title: Multi-Agent Learning Under Non-Stationary Reward Distributions
description: In a multi-agent reinforcement learning environment, each agent's reward depends on the joint actions of all agents, and the reward distribution shifts every 1000 steps as new tasks arrive and old ones expire. Design a learning algorithm where agents detect distribution shifts using change-point detection on reward statistics, adapt their policies quickly using meta-learning or policy distillation from prior experience, coordinate adaptation to avoid the thundering herd problem (all agents changing simultaneously causes instability), and maintain a portfolio of past policies for fast switching. Analyze convergence guarantees under different shift frequencies and magnitudes, and compare to iterated best response and fictitious play baselines.

title: Designing Governance Mechanisms for Autonomous Agent Collectives
description: A collective of 20 autonomous agents must make binding decisions about shared resource usage, conflict resolution, and membership (adding/removing agents). Design governance mechanisms inspired by constitutional design: a decision framework specifying which decisions require majority vs supermajority vs unanimity, amendment procedures for changing governance rules, rights and obligations for individual agents, and impeachment mechanisms for agents that consistently violate norms. Formalize using social choice theory, analyze impossibility results (Arrow's theorem implications for agent voting), design mechanisms that are robust to strategic manipulation, and address the bootstrap problem of how initial governance rules are established.

title: Hybrid Human-Agent Teams with Asymmetric Capabilities
description: Design a collaboration framework for a team of 3 human experts and 5 AI agents working together on complex analysis tasks. Humans are better at ambiguity resolution, ethical judgment, and creative synthesis but are slow and intermittently available. Agents are fast, consistent, and always available but lack common sense and make confident errors. Design task routing that accounts for asymmetric capabilities, communication protocols that adapt to human cognitive load and attention spans, escalation mechanisms where agents recognize their limitations and defer to humans, and learning mechanisms where agents improve by observing human decisions. Address the trust calibration problem where humans either over-rely on or under-trust agent outputs.

title: Designing Evaluation for Stochastic Systems with High Variance Outputs
description: An LLM-based system produces highly variable outputs: the same input generates substantially different answers across runs, with quality varying from excellent to poor. Standard evaluation (single run per input) gives unreliable results. Design an evaluation framework that estimates the output quality distribution (not just the mean), determines the minimum number of runs per input for stable estimates using sequential analysis, separates variance due to the model from variance due to input ambiguity, and designs aggregate metrics that capture both average quality and worst-case behavior (CVaR, tail risk). Apply this to compare two systems where System A has higher mean quality but heavier left tail than System B.

title: Evaluating Faithfulness of Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
description: LLMs produce chain-of-thought reasoning that may not reflect their actual computational process (post-hoc rationalization vs faithful reasoning). Design an evaluation framework to measure faithfulness: perturb reasoning steps and measure if the final answer changes accordingly (if removing step 3 doesn't change the answer, step 3 may be unfaithful), check logical validity of each inference step, compare early-exit predictions (after k reasoning steps) with the final answer to detect reasoning that is decorative rather than functional, and measure whether models that show their work are actually more accurate than those that don't. Address the fundamental epistemological challenge of verifying internal reasoning from external observations.

title: Multi-Dimensional Evaluation with Conflicting Stakeholder Requirements
description: A deployed ML system has four stakeholder groups with conflicting evaluation priorities: end users (care about accuracy and speed), legal/compliance (care about explainability and bias), engineering (care about latency, throughput, and maintenance burden), and business (care about cost and user engagement). Design an evaluation framework that formalizes each stakeholder's requirements as constraints and objectives, identifies Pareto-optimal configurations across all dimensions, provides decision-support visualizations showing tradeoffs, and handles the political reality that stakeholder weights are themselves contested. Include a mechanism for resolving deadlocks when no configuration satisfies all constraints simultaneously.

title: Detecting and Measuring Data Contamination in LLM Benchmarks
description: Suspicion arises that an LLM has been trained on benchmark test data, inflating its scores. Design a contamination detection framework that uses canary strings (deliberately distinctive test examples whose verbatim reproduction indicates memorization), membership inference attacks adapted for text, analysis of performance patterns (suspiciously uniform accuracy across easy and hard questions), and comparison of performance on original vs rephrased test items. Address the challenge that modern LLMs may have seen benchmarks in their training data indirectly (through papers discussing them, blog posts analyzing results, or paraphrased versions). Propose a benchmark design methodology that is inherently resistant to contamination.

title: Evaluating Long-Horizon Agent Performance with Sparse Rewards
description: An agent performs 500-step tasks where success is only measurable at the end (e.g., writing a complete research paper). Intermediate evaluation is ambiguous because good exploration may look unproductive initially. Design an evaluation framework with process-based metrics (quality of intermediate artifacts, efficiency of information gathering, appropriateness of tool usage at each step), trajectory comparison methods that identify where successful and failed runs diverge, credit assignment that attributes final outcomes to specific early decisions, and early stopping criteria that predict final quality from partial trajectories. Validate that your intermediate metrics actually correlate with final outcomes using historical trajectory data.

title: Designing Evaluation for Multi-Agent System Emergent Properties
description: Individual agents in a multi-agent system pass all unit tests, but the system exhibits problematic emergent behaviors: information cascades where agents amplify each other's errors, deadlocks that only occur under specific message ordering, and performance degradation that only manifests at scale. Design an evaluation framework that detects emergent properties through interaction testing (pairwise, then small groups, then full system), chaos engineering for agent systems (random message delays, agent crashes, byzantine behavior injection), formal property checking using temporal logic specifications, and statistical analysis of system-level metrics that cannot be decomposed into individual agent metrics. Address the challenge that emergent properties are by definition not predictable from component specifications.

title: Comparative Evaluation Under Non-Identical Test Conditions
description: You need to compare Model A (evaluated on benchmark X in January 2025 with GPT-4 as judge) with Model B (evaluated on benchmark Y in June 2025 with Claude as judge) where the benchmarks overlap 60% and the judges have different biases. Design a statistical framework for fair comparison: calibrate judge scores to a common scale using shared reference items, handle benchmark differences using item response theory to estimate latent ability, account for temporal effects (benchmark contamination may increase over time), and provide uncertainty bounds that reflect the comparison's limitations. Formalize the assumptions required for valid comparison and test them empirically.

title: Evaluating Model Safety Across Cultural and Linguistic Contexts
description: A safety evaluation suite developed primarily in English with Western cultural norms must be extended to 15 languages and cultural contexts. Design an evaluation framework that identifies culture-specific safety concerns (topics, norms, sensitivities that vary by culture), translates evaluation rubrics while preserving semantic intent, recruits and trains culturally diverse annotators, handles the challenge that some safety concepts don't translate directly across cultures, and aggregates results across cultures without imposing a single cultural standard. Address the meta-ethical question of whose safety norms should take priority when they conflict, and how evaluation results should inform model behavior in different deployment regions.

title: Dynamic Benchmark Generation to Prevent Overfitting to Static Tests
description: Static benchmarks become saturated and gamed over time. Design a dynamic benchmark generation system that continuously creates new test items using template-based generation with controlled difficulty, LLM-generated questions validated by human experts, adversarial item generation targeting known model weaknesses, and procedural generation for tasks with formal specifications (math, logic, code). Ensure generated items are valid (have correct answers), calibrated (known difficulty), and diverse (cover the capability space uniformly). Address the chicken-and-egg problem that you need a good model to generate good test items but the test items are meant to evaluate models. Design statistical methods to ensure scores on dynamic benchmarks are comparable across time periods.

title: End-to-End Evaluation of Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Attribution
description: Design a comprehensive evaluation framework for a RAG system that measures not just answer quality but the entire pipeline: retrieval precision and recall, generation faithfulness to retrieved context (does the answer only use information from retrieved documents?), attribution accuracy (are citations correct and sufficient?), handling of unanswerable queries (does the system correctly abstain when no relevant documents exist?), and robustness to retrieval failures (does generation quality degrade gracefully when retrieval returns irrelevant results?). Create evaluation datasets with ground-truth attribution labels, design automated attribution checking using NLI models, and measure the correlation between each pipeline component's quality and end-to-end user satisfaction. Address the challenge that human evaluators often cannot verify attributions without domain expertise.
